role_hints.oews_basis: 41-3021 Insurance Sales Agents 34.6% of wage bill (5-digit 52421 mix — clean, code-specific)
role_hints.note: Quoting, application data entry, comparison shopping across carriers and proposal assembly automate heavily (AI quoting/submission tools are live); relationship selling, complex commercial risk placement and renewal retention conversations persist. Personal-lines producers are far more exposed than middle-market commercial producers — the within-role fraction should be split by line of business.

role_hints.oews_basis: 43-4051 Customer Service Representatives 7.3% + account-manager roles in the unlisted tail, est. ~8-12% more
role_hints.note: Certificates of insurance, endorsements, policy checking, renewal remarketing and carrier correspondence are the industry's biggest AI target and are automating fast; client advocacy in claims and complex service stays human.

role_hints.role: Policy processing and claims support clerks
role_hints.oews_basis: 43-9041 Insurance Claims and Policy Processing Clerks 4.3% + 43-9061 Office Clerks 1.8% + 43-6014 Secretaries 1.1%
role_hints.note: Pure document/data workflow — near-fully automatable with current AI plus carrier API connectivity.

role_hints.role: Claims adjusters and examiners (in-house at larger agencies/MGAs)
role_hints.note: Desk adjudication of routine claims automates substantially; field inspection and negotiation persist. Mostly present in MGA/TPA-flavored firms within the code.
Signals the MGA/MGU sub-segment holding delegated underwriting authority; risk triage and file assembly automate, appetite judgment and carrier accountability persist.

role_hints.oews_basis: 11-1021 General and Operations Managers 8.2% + 43-1011 First-Line Supervisors of Office/Admin 2.0%
role_hints.note: Book analytics, carrier-relationship reporting and ops management automate substantially; carrier contract negotiation and producer recruitment persist.

role_hints.oews_basis: 13-1161 Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists 1.5% + long tail of admin/accounting occupations, est. remainder ~18-22%
role_hints.note: Lead generation, cross-sell campaigns, accounting/commission reconciliation and compliance filings automate near-fully.

source_hints.source: OPTIS Partners quarterly agent/broker M&A reports
source_hints.why: The standard deal-count tracker for insurance distribution M&A — documents the most active consolidators and deal-volume trends for B.

source_hints.source: MarshBerry market reports and 'Perspectives for High Performance' valuation commentary
source_hints.why: Publishes agency EBITDA multiple ranges by size/quality tier (base vs. earnout) — the sourced buy/exit multiple evidence for M.
The census of independent agencies — agency counts, revenue bands, owner demographics/age and technology adoption; directly feeds succession and adoption inputs.

source_hints.source: Reagan Consulting Organic Growth & Profitability (OGP) survey and Best Practices Study (with Big 'I')
source_hints.why: Agency margin, productivity and (recent editions) AI/automation benchmarking for well-run agencies.

source_hints.source: State producer-licensing requirements (NAIC/NIPR) and carrier appointment/commission structures
source_hints.why: Primary documents for the capture mechanism: revenue is carrier-set commission on premium, and licensed-producer requirements gate who can sell.

source_hints.source: MarshBerry / Reagan perpetuation studies (internal perpetuation vs. external sale)
source_hints.why: Agency-owner age and the documented failure rate of internal perpetuation — the succession-driver evidence.

source_hints.source: Vertafore / Applied Systems (agency management system vendors) technology adoption reports
source_hints.why: The two dominant AMS vendors survey agencies on automation/AI feature adoption — closest thing to an adoption time series.

capture_questions: Agency revenue is a carrier-set commission (roughly 10-15% of premium) — the client pays the carrier, not the agency, so AI cost cuts cannot be competed away through client fee pressure in the core model: does the operator therefore keep AI savings as margin dollar-for-dollar, and what are the two leaks — carriers trimming commission rates over time, and fee-based large-commercial accounts where clients do see and negotiate the cost?

Who owns the client — the agency (it legally owns expirations/renewals, the industry's core asset doctrine) or the individual producer (books walk despite non-piracy agreements)? What do acquirer retention rates say about how much of a purchased book actually renews?

What stops insureds from bypassing the agent — direct carriers (GEICO/Progressive precedent in personal lines) and embedded/AI-native distribution already exist, so the question is line-specific: why has independent-agency share held in commercial lines, and does AI finally give carriers/insurtechs the tool to underwrite small-commercial direct?

Do carriers capture the AI margin upstream — cutting commission schedules or pushing service work down to agencies without compensation — as their own AI reduces what they need agents for (data collection, first-line underwriting)?

Recurring renewal commissions make agency EBITDA unusually durable — but with 3,236 band firms and a decade-long buyer frenzy (Acrisure, Hub, BroadStreet, World, Inszone, PCF et al.), have entry multiples already converged toward platform exit multiples, killing the arbitrage the roll-up model needs? Quote MarshBerry/OPTIS current numbers rather than assuming a spread.
terminal_value_question: Distinguish AI cutting agency costs (service/processing labor — big, real) from AI replacing the agency's product (matching insureds to carriers): the mechanism holding paid demand is the independent agency's ownership of renewals plus licensed-intermediary requirements and carrier appetite fragmentation — but none of that is a statute protecting the channel itself; personal lines already proved direct distribution wins when the interaction is simple enough (GEICO), so the terminal question is whether AI makes small-commercial and middle-market placement simple enough for carriers and AI-native platforms to go direct, collapsing the commission pool the roll-up is buying. What share of the target book is complexity-protected commercial risk vs. commoditizable personal/small-commercial lines?
special_notes: This is the most consolidator-crowded services vertical in America (Acrisure, Hub, BroadStreet, Gallagher, World, Inszone, PCF, Keystone, plus PE-backed platforms in the hundreds) — usable for B and M only, never as evidence for V or A (rule 2). The strategic implication for B is crowding: verify how many of the 3,236 band firms remain independent and unapproached.

Multiples discipline: MarshBerry/OPTIS document that agency multiples rose for a decade (base multiples for quality mid-size agencies reached low-double-digit EBITDA at peak, with earnouts on top) — the buy-side is NOT cheap; source current-tier multiples and model the entry-exit spread honestly rather than assuming main-street discounts. Small (<$1M revenue) agencies still trade lower — tier the multiple by size with sources.

EBITDA normalization: owner comp inside SDE at the small end; contingent/profit-sharing commissions (carrier bonuses) are volatile and buyers haircut them; premium-market hardness/softness swings commission revenue with pricing cycles — normalize contingents and note where we sit in the P&C rate cycle before trusting TTM EBITDA.

Heterogeneity inside the code: retail P&C agencies, benefits/employee-benefits agencies, life/annuity shops, personal-lines-only agencies, MGAs/MGUs and wholesale brokers all sit in 524210 — economics and AI exposure differ (MGAs carry underwriters; benefits agencies face different disruption). Score the dominant sub-segment (retail independent P&C/benefits agencies) and note the mix; the 4-digit-level surveys (Big 'I', Reagan) cover exactly the retail-agency slice.

Boundary confusion: direct carriers and their captive-agent forces are in 5241 (carriers), TPAs/claims administrators are 524292, and title agencies are 524127 — 'insurance distribution' market sizes often blend them; also QCEW/SUSB place some captive-agent operations here. Check coverage of any benchmark against the independent-agency definition.

Winner-anchor discipline: the wage bill is white-collar and heavily automatable on paper (~30%+ pure processing/service), which tempts an inflated V — but revenue is commission on premium (price set upstream), so the value of automation is margin expansion on a fixed revenue pool, not capacity to sell more hours. Keep the V story consistent with the commission mechanics.

Licensing: every producer must hold state licenses (NIPR); AI cannot legally bind coverage or advise on it in most states without a licensed human — a real but shallow moat (carriers hold licenses too and can go direct); note it in pi_moat without overweighting.
